title: The Second Sunday of Easter
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sermon Date: April 24, 2022

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Second Sunday of Easter, also called Low Sunday, falling within the Easter Season (Eastertide) of the church calendar. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the appointed readings would likely include Acts 4:32-35, Psalm 133, 1 John 1:1-2:2, and John 20:19-31 (the account of the risen Jesus appearing to the disciples, including Thomas's doubt and faith). This Sunday marks the transition from Easter's primary celebration into the ongoing season of resurrection witness and apostolic testimony.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in early 2022 was navigating post-pandemic reopening with renewed attention to mission and reconciliation. The Diocese of Colorado (which includes Denver) had recently completed its transition under Bishop Kym Lucas's leadership, who became diocesan bishop in 2019; Lucas was actively addressing church growth and community engagement across the Front Range. General Convention 2021 had concluded the previous summer with decisions on liturgical revision and reaffirmation of LGBTQ+ inclusion, though the church remained divided on theological issues.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing springtime growth and recovery from pandemic restrictions; the city had recently celebrated the Colorado Rockies' home opener on April 8. The Metro Denver housing market was in a frenzy with historically low inventory and rising prices amid post-pandemic migration to Colorado. Local news focused on spring weather patterns (the region had experienced significant snowfall in early April) and ongoing state legislative sessions addressing education funding and water management.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The top story was the ongoing war in Ukraine, now two months old following Russia's February 24 invasion; Ukrainian forces had achieved surprising success in reclaiming territory around Kyiv and the narrative was shifting toward atrocities and potential war crimes. Domestically, the Biden administration was dealing with inflation concerns and gas prices rising sharply; the Federal Reserve was preparing for interest rate hikes. The Supreme Court leaked draft decision on Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (overturning Roe v. Wade) would break publicly just days later on May 2, so abortion rights were not yet the dominant national story, though it was brewing beneath the surface.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Beyond Ukraine, the world was watching North Korea's nuclear weapons testing resumption; Kim Jong Un's regime conducted multiple missile tests in April as U.S.-North Korea tensions spiked. In China, Shanghai was under strict COVID-19 lockdown affecting 25+ million residents, causing global supply chain and economic concerns. The U.K. was embroiled in the "Partygate" scandal with Boris Johnson facing parliamentary pressure over rule-breaking gatherings at Downing Street during lockdowns.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late April 2022 reflected a transitional anxiety: the acute crisis of the pandemic was fading but inflation and war anxiety were rising. There was a sense of tentative return to normalcy (Easter gatherings, in-person services) mixed with uncertainty about economic stability and geopolitical security. The Oscars ceremony had occurred on March 27 with Will Smith's slap of Chris Rock dominating popular discourse; meanwhile, streaming and entertainment continued fragmenting cultural attention. For Colorado specifically, the mood was one of optimism about outdoor recreation and growth, though with underlying concerns about affordability and climate.
